questions.question: What is the 7-step action plan framework for building psychological flexibility?

options.text: Just think positive and everything will work out
(1) Identify values/motivation, (2) Assess baseline, (3) Choose focus domains, (4) Select core practices, (5) Identify barriers/solutions, (6) Create 4-week plan, (7) Commit and track
No structure needed—random practice is equally effective
Complete all lessons once and you're done forever

questions.explanation: The 7-step framework synthesizes course learning into actionable practice: (1) Identify WHY (values + motivation), (2) Assess baseline (where are you now? which lessons resonated?), (3) Choose 1-2 focus domains (anxiety, emotion, self-compassion, challenge-response, failure recovery, goal flexibility, mastery orientation), (4) Select 3-5 specific practices (daily, weekly, as-needed), (5) Identify barriers + solutions, (6) Create 4-week implementation plan (Week 1: baseline, Week 2: habit, Week 3: expand, Week 4: assess/adjust), (7) Commit with tracking. Implementation intentions (specific if-then plans) significantly increase follow-through vs. vague intentions.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what should you track weekly to measure progress?

options.text: Only outcome goals (do I feel completely better?)
Process goals (practice consistency), challenge approach rate, skill application, and subjective wellbeing—track process more than just outcomes

questions.explanation: Track: (1) Practice consistency (Did I complete daily practice? Target: 5+ days/week), (2) Challenge approach rate (How many challenges approached vs. avoided?), (3) Skill application (What situations did I use skills in? Which skills?), (4) Subjective wellbeing (1-10 flexibility rating—track trend not daily fluctuation). Process goals (did I practice?) are more controllable than outcome goals (do I feel better?). Results lag practice—celebrate practicing consistently, that's the success. Gradual upward trend matters more than week-to-week fluctuation (setbacks are normal).

questions.question: What is the evidence-based approach for managing anxiety and avoidance according to the domain-specific action plans?

options.text: Daily affirmations: 'I am fearless. Anxiety has no power over me.'
Build anxiety self-efficacy through graduated exposure + defusion + behavioral activation—approach despite anxiety, don't wait for it to disappear
Avoid all anxiety-provoking situations until you've mastered anxiety management skills
Force yourself into the most anxiety-provoking situation immediately (flooding)

questions.explanation: Evidence-aligned anxiety approach: (1) Defusion with anxiety thoughts ('I'm having the thought I'll fail'), (2) Graduated exposure ladder (start manageable, build up—not flooding), (3) Behavioral activation (approach despite anxiety—action creates confidence, not reverse). Research: exposure + acceptance-based approaches = gold standard for anxiety. NOT: positive affirmations without behavior (ineffective), avoiding until you feel ready (maintains avoidance indefinitely), or flooding (too aggressive for self-guided practice). You don't master anxiety in the abstract—you build anxiety self-efficacy BY approaching anxiety-provoking situations systematically.

questions.question: What are common obstacles to maintaining practice, and what are evidence-based solutions?

options.text: No obstacles exist if you have enough willpower

Obstacles: forgetting (solution: implementation intentions/habit stacking), expecting immediate results (solution: track process not just outcome), setbacks (solution: self-compassion + resume without self-punishment)
If you encounter obstacles, you should quit entirely
Obstacles mean the course doesn't work

questions.explanation: Common obstacles + solutions: (1) 'I forget to practice' → Implementation intentions ('After [existing habit], I will [practice]'), anchor to routine, (2) 'I don't see immediate results' → Track PROCESS (am I practicing?) not just outcome, skills compound over weeks-months, (3) 'I had setback and stopped' → Self-compassion on practice itself ('I missed a week, that's normal, I can restart now'), one setback doesn't erase progress, (4) 'Feels awkward' → All new skills feel awkward initially (that's NEW, not wrong), competence develops with practice. Implementation intentions significantly increase goal achievement vs. vague intentions.

questions.question: Create your personal 4-week commitment: What will be your PRIMARY focus domain? What are 3 specific practices you'll implement (what, when, how often)? What's one barrier you anticipate and your solution? What's the smallest valued action you'll take THIS WEEK to start building psychological flexibility?

title: Building Your Psychological Flexibility Action Plan - Quiz
